does: This paper introduces Pattern Enhanced Chain of Attack (PE-CoA), a structured framework consisting of five conversation patterns designed to construct multi-turn jailbreaks against large language models through natural dialogue. The authors systematically evaluate PE-CoA across twelve different LLMs and ten harm categories, achieving state-of-the-art jailbreak success rates. The core contribution is empirically identifying and formalizing conversation patterns that exploit structural vulnerabilities in LLMs' ability to maintain safety constraints across multi-turn interactions, while uncovering specific behavioral characteristics: models show distinct weakness profiles across different patterns, defensive improvements against one pattern do not transfer to others, and models within the same family exhibit similar failure modes.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial attacks against LLMs and jailbreaking methods, building upon existing multi-turn attack approaches that gradually bypass safety constraints through conversational context. While earlier multi-turn jailbreaking methods relied on heuristic or ad hoc exploration strategies, this paper responds to the gap in understanding the underlying structural vulnerabilities that enable such attacks. It connects to broader safety research examining how different attack vectors expose different weaknesses in safety training, and relates to work investigating whether defenses against specific attack categories generalize across different threat models or harm types.
enables: These findings establish a foundation for developing pattern-aware safety training and defense mechanisms that can account for attack diversity rather than treating jailbreaking as a monolithic problem. The identification that defenses do not generalize across patterns points toward future work in either: creating unified defense approaches robust to multiple conversation-based attack strategies, developing pattern-specific mitigations tailored to how different harm categories can be targeted, or designing safety training that explicitly incorporates diverse multi-turn conversational scenarios. The discovery of shared failure modes within model families suggests research into whether these vulnerabilities stem from common architectural or training choices.
